title: Dog Ate Peanut Butter with Xylitol: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Xylitol is highly toxic to dogs. If your dog ate peanut butter containing xylitol, this is a high-priority emergency. Seek veterinary care now.
content_html:
TL;DR: Xylitol is a highly toxic sugar substitute that can cause life-threatening low blood sugar and liver failure in dogs, requiring immediate emergency veterinary intervention.

What is xylitol and why is it dangerous for my dog?

Xylitol is a common sugar substitute found in sugar-free products, including certain specialty peanut butters, chewing gum, and sugar-free candies. While perfectly safe for human consumption, it is a potent toxin for dogs. Even small amounts can trigger a massive release of insulin, causing a life-threatening drop in blood sugar (hypoglycemia) and potential acute liver failure.

How urgent is it if my dog eats something containing xylitol?

  • Xylitol ingestion is a critical, life-threatening emergency.
  • Do not wait for symptoms to appear, as effects can begin as quickly as 10 to 60 minutes after ingestion.
  • Immediate veterinary intervention is required to stabilize blood glucose levels and protect the liver from irreversible damage.

Why should I take a photo of the product label for the veterinarian?

  • If it is safe to do so, quickly grab the product packaging and take a clear photo of the ingredient list and nutritional information.
  • The photo allows your veterinarian to calculate the exact concentration of xylitol your dog may have consumed.
  • Because different brands use varying amounts, this data is vital for determining the specific treatment protocol and medication dosages needed to save your dog’s life.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, xylitol stimulates a rapid, dose-dependent insulin release that can result in profound hypoglycemia. Signs of hypoglycemia, including vomiting, weakness, ataxia, depression, hypokalemia, seizures, and coma, can develop within 30 minutes of ingestion or may be delayed up to 12-18 hours if absorption is slowed. Dosages of xylitol >75-100 mg/kg have been associated with hypoglycemia, while dosages >500 mg/kg may lead to severe hepatic insufficiency or failure. Emesis should ideally be attempted only under veterinary supervision in asymptomatic animals, and activated charcoal is not recommended. Animals ingesting >75-100 mg/kg of xylitol should be hospitalized for blood glucose monitoring every 1-2 hours for at least 12 hours; those ingesting >500 mg/kg should also have baseline and serial liver values evaluated every 24 hours for at least 72 hours. Hypoglycemia should be managed with IV dextrose boluses and/or constant-rate infusions, and dextrose should be administered even to normoglycemic dogs ingesting xylitol at >500 mg/kg, along with considering hepatoprotectants.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency, Internal Medicine

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2969)

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of xylitol poisoning?
faqs.answer: Initial symptoms often include vomiting, followed by signs of low blood sugar such as extreme lethargy, staggering, weakness, collapse, and seizures.

faqs.question: How much xylitol is toxic to a dog?
faqs.answer: Even very small amounts are dangerous. As little as 0.1 grams per kilogram of body weight can cause hypoglycemia, while higher doses can lead to liver failure.

faqs.question: Should I try to make my dog vomit at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a veterinarian or a pet poison control expert. If your dog's blood sugar is already low, inducing vomiting can be dangerous.
